title: Cleaning and conditioning a leather jacket
subtitle: Remove surface dirt and restore suppleness with the correct products for smooth leather garments
excerpt: How to clean and condition a smooth leather jacket to remove surface grime, restore suppleness, and prevent the leather from cracking with age. Covers the difference between finished and unfinished leather, choosing the right cleaner and conditioner, and the drying sequence.
sourceNotes: Saddlery Trade Federation guidance on leather care. Society of Leather Technologists and Chemists (SLTC) leather care publications. Connolly Hide Care product notes (reference format).

glossaryTerms.definition: Leather that has been coated with a pigment or lacquer layer during tanning, giving it a smooth, uniform surface that resists water and staining. Most garment leather is finished. Finished leather can be cleaned with a gentle leather cleaner; conditioners sit on the surface coating rather than penetrating the fibre. Distinguishable by the water drop test: a drop of water beads on finished leather rather than soaking in.

An oil-tanned leather (aniline or waxed) that shows a lighter mark when the surface is stretched or scratched — the lighter patch 'pulls up' when rubbed. Pull-up leather is penetrable by oils and conditioners and must not be cleaned with water-based cleaners, which strip the oils and darken the leather unevenly. Condition only with a wax or oil-based product recommended for aniline or pull-up leather.

content.text: A smooth leather jacket needs cleaning once or twice a year and conditioning at the same time. The process for
(which covers the large majority of garment leather) is: gentle cleaner, dry naturally, then conditioner. The process for

needs dry wiping only (no water cleaner), then an oil or wax conditioner. The most common mistake is using household cleaning products. Saddle soap is too alkaline for garment leather. Water-based shoe cleaners may contain silicone that blocks future conditioning.

A pH-balanced leather cleaner suitable for finished garment leather (check the product is specifically for smooth garment leather, not suede or nubuck). A leather conditioner (lanolin-based conditioners are gentle and widely suitable; wax-based conditioners give more water resistance but can darken the colour slightly). Two clean soft cloths. A clean dry towel. A hanger (the jacket must dry on a hanger, not flat).

Do the water drop test: put a small drop of water on a hidden section (inside collar seam area). If it beads: finished leather; proceed with a water-based leather cleaner. If it absorbs instantly and darkens: aniline or pull-up leather; do not use a water-based cleaner; clean with a dry lint-free cloth only and condition with a wax-based product.

Apply a small amount of leather cleaner to one cloth. Work in small sections (one panel at a time) wiping in circular motions and then straight strokes along the surface. Use moderate pressure; do not scrub. Wipe off the lifted dirt with the clean side of the cloth. Work from collar to hem, then sleeves. Pay attention to collar, cuffs, and any areas that contact skin regularly; these are where sweat and body oils accumulate.

Hang the jacket on a broad-shouldered hanger (a narrow wire hanger deforms the shoulders) in a warm room, away from direct heat and direct sunlight. Allow to dry completely before conditioning; 30 to 60 minutes in a warm room. Conditioning over damp leather traps moisture and can cause mould or softening of the leather surface coating.

Apply a small amount of leather conditioner to a clean cloth; less than you think is needed. Work the conditioner into the leather in circular motions, section by section. The conditioner should absorb without leaving a greasy residue; if the surface remains greasy after 5 minutes, you have applied too much. Allow the conditioner to absorb for at least 20 minutes, then buff with a clean dry cloth. The leather should feel supple and slightly more pliable than before. Hang to air for an hour before wearing.

attrs.symptom: Dark patches remaining on finished leather after cleaning
attrs.cause: Stain has penetrated the surface coating of the leather.
attrs.fix: Surface cleaning cannot remove stains that have penetrated the coating. A specialist leather care product containing a mild solvent may help; otherwise a leather restoration specialist can re-pigment the affected area.

attrs.symptom: Conditioner leaves a white haze on the leather surface
attrs.cause: Too much conditioner applied, or conditioner not compatible with the leather coating.
attrs.fix: Buff vigorously with a dry cloth. If the haze persists, wipe with a cloth dampened with the gentlest possible cleaner (distilled water only) and buff immediately dry.
